config.topic: Will AI cause net job losses in the next decade?

contradictions.sharedTopic: Net job losses vs. gains from AI in the next decade
AI causing unemployment rate increase vs. historical tech creating labor demand
Whether AI reduces labor demand (A says yes, B argues AI as complement undermines this)
Whether technological advancement leads to job elimination (A's cycle vs. B's historical pattern)
AI capabilities causing job displacement in the economy
Whether traditional job redeployment strategies work with technological advancement
Task composition change and worker ability to transition to new roles
Whether new AI-created roles offset job losses (net negative vs. net positive outcome)
New industries/roles from technological change and their wage characteristics
Whether technological revolutions eliminate jobs or create new labor demand

revisions.reasoning: Citrini explicitly frames analyses as stress-testable models rather than defended predictions and states hope to be proven wrong, indicating high epistemic openness to evidence. However, their financial stake in the Dynamic AI basket and subscriber-dependent business model creates moderate rigidity, but this is substantially overridden by their clearly articulated flip conditions: the opposing claims about productivity gains generating new industries, technological complementarity to labor, and policy interventions directly address the mechanisms Citrini said would make them 'glad to be incorrect.'

Flight's empiricist epistemology and reliance on real-time data creates genuine openness to revision, but Citadel's structural incentives against displacement narratives create substantial stakes rigidity. None of the opposing claims directly trigger his stated flip conditions (sustained white-collar deterioration + accelerated AI adoption + specific sector reversals in job postings/unemployment), though claims about white-collar displacement and wage decline approach the threshold without crossing it into actionable data signals.

nodes.claim: [Whether productivity shocks from technological advances follow historical patterns of job creation] AI will cause net job losses in the next decade, particularly in white-collar work, with displaced workers unable to transition to new roles and experiencing wage decline. vs. AI advancement/proliferation causes/supports unemployment rate increase vs. AI capabilities in white-collar work causes/supports job displacement in US economy vs. AI being general intelligence improving at human tasks causes/supports traditional job redeployment strategy failing vs. AI will not cause net job losses in the next decade because historical technological revolutions create productivity gains that generate new industries and labor demand, despite near-term displacement and distributional challenges. vs. technological revolutions undermines/reduces labor elimination vs. Microsoft Office advent causes/supports office worker labor demand vs. major technological advances causes/supports productivity shocks pattern vs. technological revolutions causes/supports task composition alteration

[AI capability causing economic disruption and productivity shocks] single GPU cluster replacing 10,000 white-collar workers causes/supports economic disruption vs. AI-driven automation causes/supports productivity shock

[Whether AI reduces labor demand (A says yes, B suggests AI as complement undermines this)] AI capabilities improvement undermines/reduces company demand for workers vs. AI capability in management and oversight tasks causes/supports displaced workers unable to move to AI management roles vs. AI as complement to labor undermines/reduces labor demand reduction

[Labor's economic position under high substitution between capital and labor] AI-driven displacement spiral causes/supports structural impairment of white-collar earnings power vs. extremely high elasticity of substitution between AI capital and labor causes/supports labor's share of income collapse
headcount cuts and AI tool investment causes/supports maintaining output with lower costs

[Whether new industries/roles from technological change offset job losses] AI creating new roles while rendering dozens obsolete causes/supports net negative economic outcome for workers vs. new AI-created roles paying fraction of old job wages causes/supports worker economic welfare decline vs. displaced white-collar workers taking lower-paying service/gig jobs causes/supports increased labor supply in service and gig sectors vs. increased labor supply in service and gig sectors causes/supports wage compression in those sectors vs. productivity gains causes/supports new industry generation
rising software engineer job postings (up 11% YoY) causes/supports undermines displacement narrative
marginal cost of compute rises above marginal cost of human labor undermines/reduces labor substitution by automation
persistent failure of redistribution mechanisms and simultaneous drying up of investment opportunities causes/supports structural fall in aggregate demand
physical capital constraints undermines/reduces economic deployment of automation
energy availability constraints undermines/reduces economic deployment of automation

cruxes.settlingQuestion: What will be the net change in total employment (employed persons) in the United States between 2024 and 2034, measured as a percentage change in the Bureau of Labor Statistics' total nonfarm employment figure?

cruxes.settlingQuestion: What percentage of workers displaced by AI automation over the next 10 years find new employment in roles with median wages at or above their previous position, broken down by education level and occupational category?

cruxes.claim: [Whether AI reduces labor demand (A says yes, B suggests AI as complement undermines this)] AI capabilities improvement undermines/reduces company demand for workers vs. AI capability in management and oversight tasks causes/supports displaced workers unable to move to AI management roles vs. AI as complement to labor undermines/reduces labor demand reduction
cruxes.settlingQuestion: What percentage of workers displaced by AI automation between 2020-2025 successfully transitioned to AI management, oversight, or complementary roles versus those who experienced sustained unemployment or underemployment?

decisionFlipScore.explanation: If historical technological revolutions do NOT follow patterns of job creation and productivity gains, then Citadel's entire argument collapses, and Citrini's prediction of net job losses becomes the coherent conclusion. Conversely, if the assumption holds true, it directly supports Citadel's position that new industries and labor demand will emerge despite displacement.
